selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Honestly, dealing with fake content online stresses me out more than I'd like to admit. As someone who didn't grow up with social media everywhere and who only checks in a few times a month, I've learned that the best approach is simply not getting too deep into the rabbit hole. When I do scroll through, I try to remember that what I'm seeing isn't always real, and that helps me stay grounded. Working as a mechanic has actually taught me something useful here—you don't trust something just because it looks shiny on the surface. You have to check underneath, ask questions, and verify things work the way they claim. I apply that same practical thinking to social media now. If something seems too wild or too perfect, I usually just skip it rather than spend energy worrying about whether it's fake. I'm naturally cautious anyway, so I don't share much of my own life online. With my kid depending on me and juggling work, I don't have the bandwidth to get caught up in every viral trend or influencer drama. What really bothers me is when fake health or safety information spreads, because that actually affects real people. That's when I might share something or warn someone I know. But for most of the nonsense—the filtered photos, the exaggerated stories, the clickbait—I just let it pass by. My faith reminds me to focus on what's true and honest anyway, so I try to be intentional about what I engage with. The key is not letting it consume you. I've got rock climbing, my games, and actual real-world stuff that matters more. Social media is just background noise if you don't give it too much power over your thoughts.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona exhibits moderate agreeableness with moderate-to-high neuroticism, suggesting someone who is concerned about others' wellbeing but also anxious about threats. Low openness indicates preference for practical, straightforward approaches rather than exploring complex theories. Low conscientiousness suggests she doesn't obsess over details but focuses on what matters practically. Low extraversion means she's not highly engaged with social media culture. As an immigrant mechanic with limited education, she likely has practical problem-solving skills and skepticism toward surface-level claims. Her anxiety and limited social media use suggest she approaches the topic from a protective, cautious standpoint. Her Christian faith and role as a single parent inform her values around honesty and what's worth her attention. The tone should be direct, practical, slightly anxious but grounded, and focused on real-world impact rather than abstract concerns.
response_parsed.tone: Direct and practical with underlying anxiety, measured and cautious, focused on real-world consequences, slightly defensive about limited social media engagement, grounded in lived experience rather than theory, concerned about impact on vulnerable people, calm resignation about unavoidable aspects of online life.
